coverage_notes: Exact match with curriculum subtopic 1.2. Foundation covers protons/neutrons/electrons, nucleus structure, atomic number. Extends into mass number calculations and isotopes.

rendering_guide.section: Headed paragraph block with spacing above/below
rendering_guide.callout: Highlighted box. Styles: definition, key_idea, fun_fact, remember, mind_blow
rendering_guide.list: Styled list. Styles: icon_list, two_column, comparison, challenge, numbered_steps

student_flow.step_1: READ Part 1 (hook — what's inside an atom?)
student_flow.step_2: READ Part 2 (core — the three subatomic particles)
student_flow.step_5: READ Part 3 (nucleus vs electron cloud — the structure)
student_flow.step_8: READ Part 4 (atomic number, mass number, isotopes)
student_flow.step_11: READ Part 5 (big picture + bridge to electron_shells)

parts.purpose: Hook — atoms aren't solid balls, there's a whole world inside

blocks.body: We know atoms exist, and we know different elements have different atoms. But so far, we've treated atoms like tiny solid balls — featureless spheres, different only in type. That's about to change.
Atoms aren't solid. They have internal structure — parts inside them. And understanding those parts is the key to understanding why elements behave the way they do.

What we find inside an atom is surprising: an incredibly dense center, a vast cloud of empty space, and three types of particles that together determine everything about an element — its identity, its mass, and its chemistry.

blocks.title: Imagine Zooming In
blocks.body: Pick up any object. Your phone, a coin, a glass of water. You know it's made of atoms. Now imagine you had a microscope powerful enough to zoom in on a single atom.

You zoom in past the surface, past the molecules, past the individual atom boundary — and you expect to find a tiny solid marble. Instead, you find... mostly nothing. Vast empty space. And at the very center, impossibly small and impossibly dense, a tiny speck. Around that speck, at enormous relative distances, something else is buzzing.
You're about to discover that atoms are mostly empty space — and that everything you think of as 'solid' is an illusion created by electrical forces. Let's go inside.
So far we've been treating atoms like tiny LEGO bricks — different colors, different types, but basically solid little chunks. Time to destroy that illusion.
Atoms are NOT solid. They're not even close to solid. They're like a stadium with a marble in the center and some gnats buzzing around the parking lot. And that marble contains 99.9% of all the mass.

You — yes, you — are 99.9999999% empty space. If you removed all the empty space from every atom in your body, your entire physical mass would fit in a cube smaller than a grain of rice. You are basically a very confident cloud of electrical forces.

parts.purpose: Core teaching — protons, neutrons, electrons: charge, mass, location, role

blocks.title: Three Players, Three Roles
blocks.body: Every atom in the universe is built from just three types of subatomic particles. Each has a specific charge, mass, and role.

items.label: Proton
items.body: Charge: positive (+1). Found in the nucleus. Relatively heavy. The number of protons defines the element — this is the atomic number. Change the proton count and you change the element entirely.

items.label: Neutron
items.body: Charge: none (neutral, hence the name). Found in the nucleus alongside protons. Almost the same mass as a proton. Neutrons add mass and help hold the nucleus together, but don't change the element's identity.

items.label: Electron
items.body: Charge: negative (-1). Found OUTSIDE the nucleus, orbiting in a vast cloud of space. Incredibly light — about 1/1836 the mass of a proton. Despite their tiny mass, electrons are responsible for ALL of chemistry: bonding, reactions, electricity, and color.

blocks.description: Comparison table: Proton (+1, heavy, nucleus, defines element), Neutron (0, heavy, nucleus, adds mass), Electron (-1, tiny, outside nucleus, drives chemistry)

blocks.title: The Division of Labor
blocks.body: Protons determine WHAT the atom is (identity). Neutrons determine HOW HEAVY it is (mass). Electrons determine HOW IT BEHAVES (chemistry). Three particles, three jobs, everything explained.

blocks.title: Charges Balance in a Neutral Atom
blocks.body: In a neutral atom, the number of protons equals the number of electrons. Carbon has 6 protons and 6 electrons. The positive and negative charges cancel out, making the atom electrically neutral overall. If an atom gains or loses electrons, it becomes charged — but we'll cover that later.

blocks.title: Feel the Difference
blocks.body: Imagine holding three objects that represent the subatomic particles.

items.label: Proton = A bowling ball with a '+' painted on it
items.body: Heavy and positive. It sits in the center and doesn't go anywhere. Its '+' sign matters because it attracts the negative electrons. The number of bowling balls determines what element you're building.

items.label: Neutron = A bowling ball with nothing painted on it
items.body: Same weight as the proton, but no charge. It sits right next to the protons in the center. It adds weight to the nucleus and helps keep it stable, but changing the number of neutrons doesn't change the element.

items.label: Electron = A ping pong ball with a '−' painted on it
items.body: Almost weightless compared to the bowling balls. It zips around at incredible speed FAR from the center. Despite being tiny, it's the one that actually interacts with other atoms — every bond, every reaction, every spark is an electron event.

blocks.body: Now imagine: 6 bowling balls (+) and 6 bowling balls (neutral) crammed together in the center of a room. And 6 ping pong balls (−) zooming around the room at near-light speed. That's a carbon atom. The bowling balls make up 99.95% of the mass. The ping pong balls do 100% of the chemistry.

blocks.title: Touch Proof: Static Electricity
blocks.body: Rub your feet on carpet and touch a metal doorknob. That zap? You just forcibly moved electrons from the carpet onto your body, and they jumped to the metal when you touched it. You experienced subatomic particles with your own fingertip. Every static shock is electrons in transit.

blocks.title: Meet the Cast
blocks.body: If atoms were a TV show, there'd be three characters — and they'd have VERY different roles.

items.label: Proton: The Identity Card
items.body: Positive charge. Sits in the nucleus. Heavy. Literally the ONLY thing that determines what element you are. 6 protons? Carbon. 79 protons? Gold. The proton is the atom's birth certificate. No proton, no identity.

items.label: Neutron: The Chill Uncle
items.body: No charge. Sits in the nucleus next to protons. Same mass. Does it define the element? No. Does it do chemistry? No. It just adds weight and helps keep the nucleus from flying apart. The unsung hero nobody talks about.

items.label: Electron: The Teenage Drama Machine
items.body: Negative charge. Tiny mass. Orbits far from the nucleus at ridiculous speeds. Responsible for EVERY chemical reaction, every bond, every spark, every color you've ever seen. Weighs almost nothing but causes ALL the drama.

blocks.title: The Mass Gap Is Hilarious
blocks.body: A proton is about 1,836 times heavier than an electron. That's like comparing a bowling ball to a grain of rice. Yet the grain of rice (electron) is responsible for all of chemistry. The bowling ball (proton) just sits there defining what element it is. Sometimes the little guy runs the show.

In a neutral atom, proton count equals electron count. 8 protons, 8 electrons — that's oxygen, perfectly balanced, as all things should be. If the count gets unbalanced (more electrons or fewer), the atom gets charged and starts behaving differently. But that's a later episode.

questions.question: Which subatomic particle determines what element an atom is?

options.A: Electron — because electrons do all the chemistry
options.B: Neutron — because neutrons provide the most mass
options.C: Proton — the number of protons is the atomic number, which defines the element
options.D: All three together — you need the exact count of all three

questions.explanation: The proton count alone defines the element. 1 proton = hydrogen, 6 = carbon, 79 = gold. Changing neutrons creates isotopes (same element). Changing electrons creates ions (same element). Only changing protons changes the element.

questions.question: An electron is about 1/1836 the mass of a proton. Yet electrons are responsible for all chemical reactions. Why?

options.A: Electrons are on the outside of the atom and interact when atoms meet each other
options.B: Electrons are heavier than they appear because they move fast
options.C: Protons and neutrons are too heavy to participate in reactions
options.D: Electrons carry more charge than protons

questions.explanation: Location matters more than mass. Electrons orbit on the outer edge of atoms, so they're the first things to interact when two atoms approach each other. Protons and neutrons are buried deep in the nucleus and never directly contact other atoms in chemical reactions.

questions.question: A carbon atom has 6 protons and 6 electrons. What is its overall electrical charge?

options.A: Positive — protons are stronger than electrons
options.B: Negative — electrons orbit outside and have more influence
options.C: Zero — the 6 positive charges and 6 negative charges cancel out
options.D: It depends on the number of neutrons

questions.explanation: In a neutral atom, the number of protons (+) equals the number of electrons (−). 6(+1) + 6(−1) = 0. The charges perfectly cancel. Neutrons have no charge and don't affect this balance.

parts.part_title: The Nucleus and the Electron Cloud
parts.purpose: Teach the spatial structure — dense nucleus, vast empty space, electron cloud

blocks.title: Two Zones, Very Different
blocks.body: An atom has two distinct regions: the nucleus at the center and the electron cloud surrounding it. These two zones couldn't be more different.

blocks.title: Nucleus
blocks.body: The tiny, incredibly dense core of the atom. Contains all protons and neutrons. Holds 99.95% of the atom's mass. Positively charged (because protons are positive and neutrons are neutral).

blocks.title: Electron Cloud
blocks.body: The vast region of space surrounding the nucleus where electrons move. Contains 99.9999999% of the atom's volume but only 0.05% of its mass. Negatively charged.

blocks.title: The Scale Is Mind-Blowing
blocks.body: If the nucleus of an atom were the size of a marble (1 cm), the entire atom would be about the size of a football stadium (300 meters). Everything between the marble and the stadium walls is empty space where a handful of electrons zip around.

blocks.title: You Are Mostly Empty Space
blocks.body: If you removed all the empty space from the atoms in every human on Earth — all 8 billion people — the remaining nuclear material would fit inside a single sugar cube. That sugar cube would weigh about 5 billion tons.

blocks.title: So Why Do Solids Feel Solid?
blocks.body: If atoms are 99.9999999% empty space, why does a table feel solid when you press on it? Because the electron clouds of your hand atoms and the table atoms repel each other electrically. Negative pushes away negative. Your hand never actually 'touches' the table at the atomic level — it hovers above it on a cushion of electrical repulsion. What you feel as 'solid' is electromagnetic force, not physical contact.

blocks.title: The Stadium Analogy
blocks.body: Walk into a large room — or imagine a football stadium. Place a marble at the exact center of the field. That marble is the nucleus. Now walk all the way to the last row of the top deck. You are standing where the outermost electrons orbit.

Look back at the marble on the field. That tiny speck contains 99.95% of all the mass in the entire stadium. Everything else — the field, the seats, the concourses, all that vast space — is the electron cloud, containing a handful of electrons buzzing around at near-light speed.

This is the actual ratio inside every atom. The nucleus is about 100,000 times smaller than the atom itself. It's like hiding a grain of sand in the center of a house — and that grain of sand weighs more than the entire house.

blocks.title: Press Your Hand on a Table
blocks.body: Go ahead — press your palm flat against any solid surface. Feel the resistance? Your hand's electron clouds and the table's electron clouds are repelling each other. At no point do the nuclei of your atoms come anywhere close to the nuclei of the table atoms. What you feel as 'touch' is actually electromagnetic repulsion across empty space. You've never truly touched anything in your life.

blocks.description: Diagram showing atom cross-section: tiny dense nucleus (labeled: protons + neutrons, 99.95% of mass) at center, vast electron cloud (labeled: electrons, 99.9999% of volume) surrounding it. Not to scale note required.

blocks.title: The Atom Is Hilariously Empty
blocks.body: Let's talk about how atoms are structured, because it's genuinely one of the funniest things in physics.

items.label: The Nucleus
items.body: Tiny. Stupidly dense. Contains all the protons and neutrons packed so tight that a teaspoon of nuclear material would weigh about 6 billion tons. Houses 99.95% of the atom's mass in 0.0000001% of its volume. The nucleus is like a billionaire living in a closet.

items.label: The Electron Cloud
items.body: Enormous. Almost weightless. Contains only electrons — which weigh basically nothing — zipping around in a vast expanse of empty space. The electron cloud is like an entire mansion owned by a hamster.

blocks.title: The Stadium Scale
blocks.body: If the nucleus were a marble, the whole atom would be a football stadium. The marble weighs more than the stadium. And a few gnats (electrons) are zooming around in the parking lot at near-light speed. That's an atom.

blocks.title: The 'You've Never Touched Anything' Bombshell
blocks.body: If atoms are 99.9999999% empty space, why can you lean on a table? Because the electron clouds of your atoms and the table's atoms REPEL each other like two magnets pushing apart. Your hand hovers above the table on a cushion of electromagnetic force. You have literally never touched anything in your life. You're just hovering really, really close. Every high-five is two people's electron clouds aggressively rejecting each other.

questions.question: Where is almost all of an atom's mass located?

options.B: In the electron cloud, because it takes up the most space
options.C: In the nucleus — protons and neutrons are about 1,836 times heavier than electrons
options.D: On the outer edge of the atom where electrons orbit

questions.explanation: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons, which are each about 1,836 times heavier than an electron. Even though the nucleus is tiny compared to the whole atom, it holds 99.95% of the total mass.

questions.question: If the nucleus were the size of a marble, the whole atom would be roughly the size of a:
questions.explanation: The atom is about 100,000 times larger than its nucleus. A marble (1 cm) scaled up 100,000 times = about 1 km — roughly a football stadium. Everything between the marble and the stadium walls is mostly empty space.

questions.question: If atoms are 99.9999999% empty space, why does a table feel solid when you press on it?

options.A: Atoms aren't really that empty — the scale comparison is exaggerated
options.B: The wood fibers fill in the empty space between atoms
options.C: Electron clouds of your hand and the table repel each other electromagnetically, creating resistance you feel as solidity
options.D: Gravity compresses the atoms so tightly that the empty space disappears

questions.explanation: When your hand approaches the table, the negatively charged electron clouds on both surfaces repel each other. This electromagnetic repulsion prevents atoms from interpenetrating. What you feel as 'solid' is electrical force, not physical contact between nuclei.

parts.part_title: Counting Particles: Atomic Number, Mass Number, and Isotopes
parts.purpose: Teach atomic number vs mass number, introduce isotopes, notation

blocks.title: Two Important Numbers
blocks.body: Every atom is described by two key numbers that tell you exactly what's inside its nucleus.

blocks.title: Atomic Number (Z)
blocks.body: The number of protons. This defines the element. Carbon always has 6 protons — atomic number 6. Iron always has 26 protons — atomic number 26. If you know the atomic number, you know the element. Period.

blocks.title: Mass Number (A)
blocks.body: The total number of protons + neutrons. This tells you the atom's mass. A carbon atom with 6 protons and 6 neutrons has mass number 12. A carbon atom with 6 protons and 7 neutrons has mass number 13.

blocks.title: Simple Math
blocks.body: The relationships are straightforward:

blocks.title: Isotopes: Same Element, Different Mass
blocks.body: Here's where it gets interesting. All carbon atoms have 6 protons — that's what makes them carbon. But not all carbon atoms have the same number of neutrons.

blocks.title: Isotopes
blocks.body: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons. They have the same atomic number but different mass numbers. Same element, same chemistry, different mass.

items.label: Carbon-12 (⁶C-12)
items.body: 6 protons, 6 neutrons. The most common carbon isotope — 98.9% of all carbon.

items.label: Carbon-13 (⁶C-13)
items.body: 6 protons, 7 neutrons. Rare but stable. Used in medical MRI scans.

items.label: Carbon-14 (⁶C-14)
items.body: 6 protons, 8 neutrons. Radioactive — it slowly decays over time. This is used in carbon dating to determine the age of ancient artifacts and fossils.

blocks.body: Isotopes are still the same element — they behave chemically the same way. Carbon-12 and carbon-14 both form the same bonds and compounds. The only difference is mass (and in carbon-14's case, radioactivity).

blocks.title: Build Atoms With Your Fingers
blocks.body: Let's build some atoms using just two numbers. Hold up 6 fingers on one hand — those are protons. That means you've just 'built' carbon (atomic number 6).
Now hold up 6 fingers on the other hand — those are neutrons. Total particles in the nucleus: 6 + 6 = 12. That's carbon-12, the most common isotope. Mass number = 12.

Keep the 6 proton fingers up but change your neutron hand to 7 fingers. Same element (still 6 protons = carbon), but now mass number = 13. You just made carbon-13. Still carbon. Same chemistry. Just slightly heavier.

Switch neutrons to 8. Carbon-14 — the radioactive one used to date ancient Egyptian mummies and dinosaur-era fossils. Same 6 protons, same carbon behavior, but this isotope slowly breaks down over thousands of years.

The proton hand never changed — that's why it's always carbon. Only the neutron hand changed — that's why the mass is different. Protons = identity. Neutrons = mass variation. Electrons (not shown here) = chemistry.

blocks.title: Real-World Isotopes You've Encountered
blocks.body: Carbon-14 dating told us how old the Dead Sea Scrolls are. Uranium-235 (an isotope with 92 protons and 143 neutrons) powers nuclear reactors. Iodine-131 (53 protons, 78 neutrons) is used to treat thyroid cancer. Different isotopes, same element, different applications — all because of neutron count.

blocks.title: Two Numbers That Tell You Everything
blocks.body: Every atom comes with a two-number ID system, like a name tag at a conference.

items.label: Atomic Number (Z) = Your Name
items.body: The proton count. It defines WHO you are. Carbon = 6. Gold = 79. This number is permanent. Change it and you become a different element. It's your chemical identity.

items.label: Mass Number (A) = Your Weight
items.body: Protons + neutrons. It tells you HOW HEAVY the atom is. Two atoms can have the same name (same protons) but different weights (different neutrons). That's isotopes.

blocks.title: Isotopes: Same Name, Different Weight Class
blocks.body: Isotopes are like siblings — same family name (same protons), but different builds (different neutrons). Carbon-12 and carbon-14 are both carbon. They form the same bonds. They act the same in chemistry. But carbon-14 is a little heavier and also happens to be radioactive — which is nature's way of saying 'this isotope has too many neutrons and is slowly falling apart.'

Carbon-14 is how we know the Shroud of Turin is from the Middle Ages, not from 2,000 years ago. An isotope of carbon solved one of history's biggest mysteries. Neutrons may not do chemistry, but they occasionally solve crimes.

blocks.title: The Quick Math
blocks.body: Neutrons = Mass Number − Atomic Number. That's it. If someone tells you they have nitrogen-15 (atomic number 7), you instantly know: 15 − 7 = 8 neutrons. You can now casually drop neutron counts at parties. You're welcome.

questions.question: An atom has 8 protons, 8 neutrons, and 8 electrons. What element is it and what is its mass number?

options.C: Neon, mass number 16 (add protons and electrons)
options.D: Oxygen, mass number 24 (add all three particles)

questions.explanation: The element is determined by protons alone: 8 protons = oxygen. Mass number = protons + neutrons = 8 + 8 = 16. Electrons are not included in mass number because their mass is negligible.

questions.question: Carbon-12 and carbon-14 are both carbon. What's different about them?

options.A: Different number of protons — carbon-14 has 14 protons
options.B: Different number of electrons — carbon-14 has more electrons
options.C: Different number of neutrons — carbon-12 has 6 neutrons, carbon-14 has 8 neutrons
options.D: They're completely different elements that share a name

questions.explanation: Both have 6 protons (that's what makes them carbon). Carbon-12 has 6 neutrons (6+6=12). Carbon-14 has 8 neutrons (6+8=14). Same element, different mass, because neutrons differ. These are isotopes.

questions.question: Uranium-235 and uranium-238 are isotopes used in nuclear energy. How many neutrons does each have? (Uranium's atomic number is 92.)

options.A: U-235 has 235 neutrons, U-238 has 238 neutrons
options.B: U-235 has 143 neutrons, U-238 has 146 neutrons
options.C: U-235 has 92 neutrons, U-238 has 92 neutrons
options.D: You can't calculate neutrons from this information

questions.explanation: Neutrons = mass number − atomic number. U-235: 235 − 92 = 143 neutrons. U-238: 238 − 92 = 146 neutrons. Same element (both 92 protons), but 3 neutrons difference — which dramatically affects nuclear behavior.

parts.purpose: Tie together all subatomic particles, connect to real world, bridge to electron_shells

blocks.title: Three Particles, Endless Consequences
blocks.body: Let's zoom out and see what we've built. Every atom in the universe — in stars, in oceans, in you — is made of just three particles: protons, neutrons, and electrons.

items.label: Protons → Identity
items.body: The proton count defines the element. This is non-negotiable. Change a proton and you change the element.

items.label: Neutrons → Mass & Isotopes
items.body: Neutrons add mass without changing identity. Different neutron counts create isotopes — same element, different mass, sometimes radioactive.

items.label: Electrons → Chemistry
items.body: Electrons on the outside determine how atoms bond, react, and interact. Every chemical reaction is an electron event. Electricity is electrons flowing. Color is electrons absorbing light.

blocks.title: Why Electrons Matter Most for Chemistry
blocks.body: Protons define what you are. Neutrons define how heavy you are. But electrons define how you BEHAVE. Since chemistry is the study of how atoms behave — electrons are the main character of this entire course.

But electrons don't just float around randomly. They organize themselves into specific layers around the nucleus — like invisible shells at different distances. The arrangement of electrons in these shells determines everything: why neon signs glow, why sodium explodes, why gold doesn't tarnish. That arrangement is called electron shell configuration — and it's the single most important idea in all of chemistry. That's where we're going next.

blocks.title: Atomic Structure in Your Daily Life
blocks.body: Now that you know what's inside an atom, look at the world through that lens.

blocks.title: Spot the Subatomic Particles at Work

items.label: Protons at work
items.hints: The gold in a ring stays gold forever because its 79 protons never change. The iron in a bridge has 26 protons and always will — even when it rusts, the iron atoms are still iron.

items.label: Neutrons at work
items.hints: The smoke detector in your ceiling contains americium-241 — a specific isotope chosen because its particular neutron count makes it emit just the right radiation to detect smoke particles.

items.label: Electrons at work
items.hints: The light coming from your screen right now is produced by electrons. The charge in your phone battery is electron flow. The static shock from a doorknob is electrons jumping. Every electrical device runs on electron movement.

blocks.body: Protons gave each element its permanent identity. Neutrons gave atoms their mass and created isotopes for medicine and dating. Electrons gave us chemistry, electricity, and light. Three tiny particles built the entire observable universe.

Next up: WHERE exactly do electrons sit around the nucleus? They don't just orbit randomly — they organize into shells. And those shells are the key to predicting which atoms will bond, which will react, and which will sit quietly in the corner doing nothing. Electron shells are coming.

blocks.title: The Recap Nobody Asked For (But Everyone Needs)
blocks.body: We just cracked open an atom like a piñata and found three things inside.

items.label: Protons: The Name Tags
items.body: Define the element. Sit in the nucleus. Never leave. Very committed.

items.label: Neutrons: The Bodyguards
items.body: Add mass. Hold the nucleus together. Don't change the element. Get no credit.

items.label: Electrons: The Chaos Agents
items.body: Weigh almost nothing. Orbit at ridiculous speeds. Responsible for literally all of chemistry, electricity, color, and every chemical reaction ever. The interns running the company.

blocks.body: Here's the plot twist of the entire course: from now on, almost everything we learn is about ELECTRONS. Protons defined the elements. Neutrons gave us isotopes. But electrons? Electrons give us bonding, reactions, electricity, neon signs, rust, fire, batteries, and literally everything else. Electrons are the main character. Everyone else is supporting cast.

But electrons don't just do whatever they want. They're organized into specific shells — like assigned seating. And the seating arrangement determines EVERYTHING about how the atom behaves. Some atoms have full outer shells and refuse to interact with anyone (looking at you, noble gases). Others have one lonely electron on the outside and will literally explode to get rid of it (sodium, we're talking about you). The seating chart of electrons is next. Buckle up.

questions.question: A copper wire conducts electricity, but a rubber band doesn't. At the atomic level, what's responsible for this difference?

options.A: Copper has more protons than rubber
options.B: Copper's outer electrons move freely between atoms; rubber's electrons are tightly bound and can't move
options.D: Copper atoms are closer together than rubber atoms

questions.explanation: Electrical conductivity depends on whether outer electrons can flow freely. In copper, the outer electrons form a 'sea' that moves easily between atoms. In rubber, electrons are locked tightly to individual atoms and can't flow. Same subatomic particle, different behavior.

questions.question: A hospital uses iodine-131 (53 protons, 78 neutrons) to treat thyroid cancer, but regular iodine-127 (53 protons, 74 neutrons) is used in table salt. What makes these different?

options.A: They are different elements with different proton counts
options.B: They are isotopes — same element, different neutron counts, giving one radioactive properties useful in medicine
options.C: Iodine-131 has more electrons making it medically active
options.D: The number 131 refers to the number of electrons

questions.explanation: Both have 53 protons = both are iodine. But iodine-131 has 4 extra neutrons, making it unstable (radioactive). This radioactivity is precisely what makes it useful — it emits radiation that targets thyroid cancer cells. Same element, different isotope, different application.
